regulator: FINMA (with self-regulatory organisations recognised under the AMLA and the Money Laundering Reporting Office Switzerland, MROS)
instruments: Anti-Money Laundering Act of 10 October 1997 (Federal Act on Combating Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing in the Financial Sector, AMLA; SR 955.0) -- the technology-neutral statute FINMA has applied to virtual-currency dealings since its earliest crypto-specific practice; not a dedicated crypto-AML law, and not to be confused with the EU's Anti-Money Laundering Authority (also abbreviated AMLA), a separate EU institution covered by this site's EU-tagged glossary entry

FINMA Anti-Money Laundering Ordinance (AMLO-FINMA / Geldwäschereiverordnung-FINMA; SR 955.033.0) of 3 June 2015, Article 10 (disclosures in payment orders -- the technology-neutral analogue of the FATF travel rule), Article 51a (transactions with virtual currencies, inserted by the FINMA Ordinance of 4 November 2020 on financial institutions, in force since 1 January 2021) and Article 52 (transfer of money and securities)
FINMA Guidance 02/2019, 'Payments on the blockchain' (26 August 2019) -- FINMA's interpretive guidance applying Article 10 AMLO-FINMA to blockchain-based payments and external (unhosted) wallets

Financial Market Supervision Act (FINMASA; SR 956.1), Articles 33 (professional ban) and 35 (confiscation of profits) -- FINMA's general enforcement powers, applied to crypto-related supervisory breaches under ordinary supervisory law rather than a crypto-specific enforcement statute

Federal Act on the Transparency of Legal Entities and the Identification of Beneficial Owners (Transparency Act / TLEA) and the accompanying partial revision of the AMLA extending its scope to certain advisers (real estate transactions and the formation/structuring of legal entities) -- both adopted by the Federal Assembly on 26 September 2025 (Federal Gazette, BBl 2025 2900), referendum deadline passed unchallenged, both due to enter into force on 1 October 2026

FINMA, partial revision of the AMLO-FINMA -- consultation opened 12 May 2026 and closed 9 June 2026; addresses ownership/control-structure due diligence, measures to prevent breaches of the Embargo Act, and correspondent-banking/sub-account provisions; not yet in force

FINMA press release, 'FINMA closes down coin providers and issues warning about fake cryptocurrencies' (19 September 2017) -- illustrative early application of ordinary supervisory-law enforcement to an unauthorised virtual-currency scheme
standing_summary: The Anti-Money Laundering Act of 10 October 1997 (AMLA, SR 955.0) is a technology-neutral statute, not a crypto-specific law, and FINMA's own guidance states plainly that 'Switzerland has always applied the Anti-Money Laundering Act to blockchain service providers.' This pillar's law is therefore the same amend-and-apply pattern seen elsewhere in this jurisdiction's regime: an existing statute and its implementing ordinance, layered with FINMA interpretive guidance, rather than a dedicated crypto-AML law. (Switzerland's AMLA is a statute; it should not be confused with the European Union's Anti-Money Laundering Authority, a supervisory body that shares the same acronym and is covered separately in this site's EU-tagged glossary.) On scope: FINMA's own FinTech authorisation page states that trading with virtual currencies (Bitcoin, etc.) and operating a payment system fall under the Anti-Money Laundering Act (AMLA), and that any business subject to the AMLA 'must become a member of a self-regulatory organisation (SRO)' unless it is directly supervised by FINMA -- so virtual-asset service providers (VASPs) are financial intermediaries under AMLA Article 2 and must either join a FINMA-recognised SRO or hold a licence that brings them under FINMA's direct AML supervision; there is no VASP-specific licence category. The travel-rule mechanics sit in the FINMA Anti-Money Laundering Ordinance (AMLO-FINMA, SR 955.033.0). Article 10 AMLO-FINMA requires the originator's financial intermediary to disclose the client's name, account number/reference and address (or equivalent identifiers) together with the beneficiary's name and account number in payment orders. FINMA Guidance 02/2019, 'Payments on the blockchain' (26 August 2019), states that this obligation must be interpreted in a technology-neutral way and so also applies to services based on blockchain technology, and that transmission need not occur on-chain. Describing what the director's brief flags as Switzerland's notably strict position, the guidance states that, unlike the FATF standards, Article 10 AMLO-FINMA 'does not provide for any exception for payments involving unregulated wallet providers.' Concretely, the guidance states that a FINMA-supervised institution may only send or receive payments to or from an external (unhosted) wallet if that wallet belongs to one of its own customers, with ownership proven by suitable technical means; a transfer to or from a third party's external wallet is permitted only if the institution first identifies that third party and establishes the beneficial owner, in the same way as for a client relationship. Separately from Article 10's information-transmission duty, AMLO-FINMA sets numeric identification thresholds for occasional (non-permanent-relationship) transactions: Article 51a, titled 'Transactions with virtual currencies' (inserted by the FINMA Ordinance of 4 November 2020 on financial institutions, in force since 1 January 2021), requires identification of the contracting party once one or more interconnected virtual-currency transactions reach or exceed CHF 1,000, provided they are not money-and-securities transfers and no permanent business relationship exists; Article 52(2) separately requires identification of the recipient of an incoming cross-border money-and-securities transfer -- a category that Article 2(c) AMLO-FINMA's definition expressly includes virtual currencies within -- once such transfers reach or exceed CHF 1,000. This pillar flags precisely what the director's brief warned is frequently misstated in secondary sources: the commonly cited 'CHF 1,000 Swiss travel-rule threshold' is, on this research's direct reading of the current-at-the-source-date ordinance text, the Article 51a/Article 52 occasional-transaction identification threshold, not a numeric threshold written into Article 10's payment-order information-transmission duty itself, which FINMA Guidance 02/2019 states applies without a de minimis carve-out for wallet-provider transactions. FINMA's 2022 money-laundering supervision report confirms as current supervisory practice that year that 'the Swiss provisions governing the information to be exchanged in the course of payment transactions' -- also referred to as the Travel Rule -- also apply in the blockchain domain, and documents two accepted technical methods institutions used to prove external-wallet ownership: a time-boxing procedure and verification via clients logging into their own wallets in an employee's presence. On enforcement, this pillar found no evidence of a crypto-specific enforcement statute: breaches are pursued under ordinary supervisory law (FINMASA), the same toolkit FINMA applies across all supervised and unsupervised financial intermediaries. As a worked, primary-sourced example of that mechanism applied to an unauthorised virtual-currency scheme, FINMA's press release of 19 September 2017 states that the QUID PRO QUO Association, DIGITAL TRADING AG and Marcelco Group AG had together accepted 'at least four million Swiss francs from several hundred users' via an unauthorised deposit-taking scheme involving 'E-Coins,' had 'seriously breached supervisory law by failing to obtain the required authorisation,' and were placed into liquidation, with FINMA seizing and blocking 'assets to the value of approximately two million Swiss francs.' This research could not independently verify against a FINMA primary-source document a more recent (2024) enforcement matter, widely repeated in secondary/legal-alert sources under the anonymised name 'X Ltd,' describing unauthorised acceptance of cryptocurrency-denominated deposits from approximately 2,000 investors; it is therefore not stated as fact here and is flagged in open items. The most recent large, primary-sourced FINMA action under this same ordinary-supervisory-law/AMLA-enforcement mechanism is the 27 February 2026 liquidation of MBaer Merchant Bank AG, whose licence FINMA withdrew after finding 'serious, systematic shortcomings' in AML due diligence, that 80% of business relationships carried increased risk, that 98% of incoming assets came from high-risk clients, and specific indications the bank had helped clients circumvent official asset freezes -- included here only to illustrate the current scale and severity of the same enforcement tools this pillar describes; FINMA's own press release contains no reference to cryptocurrency or digital assets, and this pillar does not present MBaer as a digital-asset case. On trajectory: the Federal Assembly adopted, on 26 September 2025 (Federal Gazette BBl 2025 2900), both a Federal Act on the Transparency of Legal Entities and the Identification of Beneficial Owners (creating a non-public federal beneficial-ownership register) and an accompanying partial revision of the AMLA that extends AMLA's scope for the first time to certain professional advisers (real-estate transactions and the formation/structuring of legal entities); per the Swiss State Secretariat for International Finance, the referendum deadline passed unchallenged and both measures are due to enter into force on 1 October 2026. Separately, and at the ordinance rather than statute level, FINMA opened a consultation on a partial revision of AMLO-FINMA on 12 May 2026 (closed 9 June 2026); FINMA's own press release describes this revision as addressing financial intermediaries' understanding of customer ownership/control structures, new measures to prevent breaches of the Embargo Act, and correspondent-banking/transitory-account and sub-account due diligence -- this research did not find any reference to virtual assets, VASPs, or Article 51a in FINMA's own press release, and does not adopt secondary-source claims (found in law-firm and industry-blog commentary) that this specific consultation addresses crypto-custody, staking-as-a-service or DeFi-gateway definitions.

open_items: This pillar's quotation of AMLO-FINMA Articles 10, 51a and 52 is sourced from an unofficial English translation (KPMG) whose stated status is '1 January 2021.' This research found no evidence in FINMA's 12 May 2026 AMLO-FINMA consultation materials of a subsequent amendment to Articles 51a or 52, but did not independently confirm the live, current fedlex text of these specific articles against the 2021-status translation; this should be reverified directly against fedlex.admin.ch (which requires a JavaScript-enabled browser this research's fetch tooling could not render) before being treated as settled.

Several secondary sources (Notabene, Scorechain, 21Analytics and others) claim the CHF 1,000 virtual-currency identification threshold has recently been reduced toward zero to align with an FATF recommendation. This research could not find any primary FINMA or fedlex source supporting that claim and does not include it as fact.

This pillar distinguishes AMLO-FINMA Article 10's payment-order information-transmission duty (no de minimis threshold, per FINMA Guidance 02/2019) from Articles 51a/52's CHF 1,000 occasional-transaction identification thresholds, because the director's brief flagged that Switzerland's threshold and travel-rule practice are frequently conflated in secondary sources; this distinction rests on this research's own reading of the translated ordinance text and guidance document, not on a FINMA statement that explicitly draws this same distinction, and should be checked against FINMA's current consolidated guidance before being treated as FINMA's own framing.

A 2024 FINMA enforcement matter against an anonymised 'X Ltd' for unauthorised deposit-taking via cryptocurrencies, widely and consistently described in secondary/legal-alert sources (with matching figures: roughly 2,000 investors, several hundred thousand US dollars, May 2024), could not be independently verified against a FINMA primary-source press release or the FINMA Enforcement Report 2024 (published as a spreadsheet this research's tooling could not read) during this research. It is not stated as fact in this pillar and should be reverified directly against finma.ch before being cited.

The MBaer Merchant Bank AG liquidation (27 February 2026) is verified against FINMA's own press release and is included only to illustrate the scale of FINMA's ordinary-supervisory-law AML enforcement tools; FINMA's release contains no reference to cryptocurrency or digital assets, and this research found no independent confirmation that the case involves digital assets. It should not be read or cited as a digital-asset enforcement case.

Secondary sources (CMS Law, an AI-generated 'Bollwerk AI Blog' post, and others) describe the 12 May 2026 AMLO-FINMA partial-revision consultation as addressing crypto-custody, staking-as-a-service and DeFi-gateway VASP definitions, and cite an expected 1 January 2027 entry into force. FINMA's own 12 May 2026 press release, as fetched during this research, describes only ownership/control-structure due diligence, Embargo Act compliance measures, and correspondent-banking/sub-account provisions, with no expected entry-into-force date stated and no mention of virtual assets or VASPs. This pillar reports only what FINMA's own release states and flags the broader secondary-source claims as unverified.

This pillar covers FINMA/federal AML-CFT regulation and enforcement. It does not independently research the Money Laundering Reporting Office Switzerland's (MROS) reporting statistics, criminal-law money-laundering provisions under the Swiss Criminal Code (Art. 305bis/305ter, referenced only where AMLO-FINMA itself cross-references them), or SRO-level enforcement actions against their own affiliated members, which are supervised by the SROs rather than FINMA directly.

The pending AMLA revision described here (Transparency Act plus advisers now in AMLA scope, both due 1 October 2026) is a Federal Assembly-level statutory change, distinct from FINMA's ordinance-level AMLO-FINMA consultation described separately above; the two should not be conflated when tracking this pillar's trajectory.
